relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: In Uz, Job stood as a whole and upright human, turned toward the Divine and away from harm.
His life was full with seven sons and three daughters.
Abundance surrounded him until he was known as greatest among the people of the east.
His children moved in cycles of feasting and family gathering.
Job guarded the unseen heart of his household through consecration and offering.
The divine court gathered, and the Accuser appeared among the heavenly beings.
The Accuser came from roaming the earth, watching the human field.
The Eternal pointed to Job’s integrity as something rare on earth.
The Accuser questioned whether devotion exists without reward.
Protection, blessing, and abundance were named as the hedge around Job.
The Accuser claimed that loss would reveal Job’s devotion as conditional.
A boundary was set: what Job has may be touched, but Job himself is not to be touched.
The day of feasting became the day of unraveling.
The first messenger arrived with loss from the field.
Servants and animals were taken, and only one voice escaped.
Fire fell, and another surviving voice carried grief.
Raiders took the camels, and another messenger remained.
The final report turned toward the children.
A great wind collapsed the house, and Job’s children died.
Job entered mourning and bowed himself to the ground.
He answered loss with naked truth: nothing was possessed at birth, nothing is carried at death.
Through the first collapse, Job did not turn his grief into false accusation against the Divine.
